coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric and colorimetric sensor array for discrimination of glucose using enzymatic-triggered dual-signal system consisting of Au@Ag nanoparticles and carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wei
Ding, Fang
Wang, Yanying
Mao, Lei
Liang, Ruoxuan
Zou, Ping
Wang, Xianxiang
Zhao, Qingbiao
Rao, Hanbing
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, a dual-signal senor for glucose detection has been established based on Au@Ag bimetallic core-shell nanoparticles (Au@Ag NPs) and carbon nanodots (C-dots). As a donor of surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET) in this senor, the fluorescence of C-dots was quenched by silver shell (acceptor) of the Au@Ag NPs. Furthermore, Au@Ag NPs serve as colorimetric reporter for the etching effect of H2O2 produced from enzymatic-triggered oxidation of glucose on silver shell, the fluorescence of C-dots recovery. Therefore, the change of color and fluorescence of this senor are directly related to the concentration of glucose. The linear relationship between fluorescence (absorbance) intensity and glucose was in the range of 2.0–400 μM (0.50–300 μM) with a low limit of detection of 0.67 μM (0.20 μM). Besides, this dual-signal senor also exhibited excellent selectivity for distinguishing glucose from common substances. This sensor system was successfully applied to the analysis of glucose in pretreated blood sample with satisfactory results, demonstrating the application prospect for practical sample analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-signal senor
Au@Ag bimetallic core-shell nanoparticles
Surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer
Enzymatic-triggered

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence and UV–vis detection of glucose
Glucose detection in human blood sample
Characterization of the proposed senor for glucose
Sensitivity and selectivity for glucose assay
Detection of glucose in blood sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant from the Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No.03570113), the Education Department of Sichuan Provincial , PR China (Grant No. 16ZA0039 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the fluorometric and colorimetric assay for glucose detection based on Au@Ag NPs and C-dots.

a) Fluorescence spectrum of AuNPs, Au@Ag NPs, C-dots, C-dots with Au@Ag NPs and those after etched with 200 μM H2O2. b) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs, Au@Ag NPs, C-dots, C-dots with Au@Ag NPs and those after etched with 200 μM H2O2.

a) TEM and HRTEM images of C-dots. b) TEM of AuNPs. c) TEM images of Au@Ag NPs in presence of C-dots, the inset is the selected area electron diffraction pattern of Au@Ag NPs. d) TEM images of Au@Ag NPs with C-dots after H2O2 etched.
a) DLS measurement of AuNPs. b) Au@Ag NPs. c) Au@Ag NPs in presence of 200 μM H2O2.
a) Energy-dispersive (EDS) spectra of Au@Ag NPs with C-dots. b) Elemental mapping of Au@Ag NPs.

a) Fluorescence response of Au@Ag NPs with C-dots to glucose with different concentration, the inset was photographs of Au@Ag NPs with C-dots in the presence of different concentration glucose under UV light (365 nm) (left to right: 0 μM, 100 μM, 150 μM, 200 μM, 250 μM, 300 μM). b) The relationship between F/F0-1 and the concentration of glucose from 0 to 1000 μM, and the linear response for glucose is 2–400 μM. c) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au@Ag NPs with C-dots to glucose with different concentration, the inset was photographs of Au@Ag NPs with C-dots in the presence of different concentration glucose under natural light (left to right: 0 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM, 150 μM, 200 μM, 250 μM, 300 μM). d) The relationship between relative absorption intensity (A/A0) and the concentration of glucose from 0 to 1000 μM, the linear range for glucose is 0.50–300 μM.
The fluorescence (F/F0-1) and absorbance (A0/A-1) response of the Au@Ag NPs with C-dots in the presence of glucose or similar molecules and other common interfering substances.
Comparative analysis of various sensors employed for glucose detective.
The determination results of glucose in human serum.

head.ce:title: Fluorometric and colorimetric sensor array for discrimination of glucose using enzymatic-triggered dual-signal system consisting of Au@Ag nanoparticles and carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xin Kang Road, Yucheng District, Ya’an 625014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Suzhou Institute of Systems Medicine, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215123, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Systems Medicine, Institute of Basic Medical Sciences, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences & Peking Union Medical College, Beijing 100005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences & Peking Union Medical College

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices, Ministry of Education, Department of Electronic Engineering, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent of carbon nanodots (C-dots) was quenched by Au@Ag bimetallic core-shell nanoparticles (Au@Ag NPs), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) produced by the enzymatic-triggered oxidation of glucose, in the presence of H2O2, the silver shell of Au@Ag NPs was etched and turn into Ag+, as a result, the fluorescent of C-dots was recovery and the color of Au@Ag NPs was change. Thus, a dual-signal senor was established for glucose detection.

In the present study, a dual-signal senor for glucose detection has been established based on Au@Ag bimetallic core-shell nanoparticles (Au@Ag NPs) and carbon nanodots (C-dots). As a donor of surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET) in this senor, the fluorescence of C-dots was quenched by silver shell (acceptor) of the Au@Ag NPs. Furthermore, Au@Ag NPs serve as colorimetric reporter for the etching effect of H2O2 produced from enzymatic-triggered oxidation of glucose on silver shell, the fluorescence of C-dots recovery. Therefore, the change of color and fluorescence of this senor are directly related to the concentration of glucose. The linear relationship between fluorescence (absorbance) intensity and glucose was in the range of 2.0–400 μM (0.50–300 μM) with a low limit of detection of 0.67 μM (0.20 μM). Besides, this dual-signal senor also exhibited excellent selectivity for distinguishing glucose from common substances. This sensor system was successfully applied to the analysis of glucose in pretreated blood sample with satisfactory results, demonstrating the application prospect for practical sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus is known as a group of metabolic diseases related to a glucose metabolic disorder arising from insulin deficiency or resistance. The direct manifestation of diabetes is chronic hyperglycemia, which can lead to multi-system damages, including retina, hearts, nerves, kidneys, blood vessels, and other organs [1]. According to World Health Organization, over 285 million people in the world were affected with diabetes in the year 2010, and this number will double in 2030 [2]. Regular monitoring and strict control of glycemic level are key measures to prevent complications of diabetes [3]. Most samples need to be diluted before testing, and complicated blood components severely interference with the determination of glucose in the blood, thus a highly sensitive and selective method for glucose determination is vital for clinical analysis and medical diagnostics.

In recent years, various analytical methods have been proposed for glucose detection, such as chemiluminescence [4,5], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [6–8], mass spectrometry [9,10], colorimetric [11–13], electrochemical [14,15], and fluorometric methods [16,17]. Among these methods, fluorometric and colorimetric analysis have attracted increasing attention due to the characteristics of eye-naked visibly, simplicity, rapid response, and high sensitivity. Moreover, the fluorometric and colorimetric sensors typically do not require high experimental cost or sophisticated instrumentation, detection procedures and complicated operation, therefore a large number of glucose sensors have been fabricated based on fluorescent and chromogenic compounds. Ma et al. [17] proposed a fluorescence senor for rapid glucose analysis based on carbon nanodots supported on silver nanoparticles. Zhang et al. [11] established a straightforward colorimetric senor for determination of hydrogen peroxide and glucose based on the peroxidase mimics of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites. However, previous research strategies for determination of glucose only provide the function to output a single signal, which is not completely compelling because the signal readout can be serious interfered by variety of factors. In recent years, the colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal senor have attracted great attention for the highly anti-interference ability, rapidly response, and feasible record in the signal output.

Noble metal nanoparticles chemistry is a domain in rapid expansion due to the unique optical properties, especially AgNPs and AuNPs. They have drawn great attention due to their surface plasmon resonance absorption (SPR)–related characteristic tunable optical properties, thus many novel glucose colorimetric sensors have been proposed based on the unique properties of AgNPs and AuNPs [17,18]. In addition, it is confirmed that AgNPs can quench various fluorescent substance, such as QDs [19] and organic dyes [20]. However, most research mentioned previously was based on the single-element nanoparticles. It should be noted that the dual-element nanoparticles such as Au@Ag bimetallic core-shell nanoparticles (Au@Ag NPs) exhibit superior catalyst performance and the characteristic optical, electronic properties [21], and thus they have broader application prospect in analytical sensing.

As one of the ideal nanomaterial, C-dots exhibits great potential in chemical sensing and bioimaging due to unique properties such as tunable excitation and emission spectra, low cytotoxicity, superior photo stability, and fine biological compatibility [22]. The emergence of C-dots has seriously challenged the position of traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) due to free of heavy metals as well as good biocompatibility [23]. Besides, C-dots have proven to be efficient energy donors, thus they can act as a fluorescence reporter in the sensing system. Shen et al. [24] successfully synthesized the B-doped carbon quantum dots which were used as fluorescent indicators and developed the glucose sensors system. However as far as we know, almost all previous strategies only possessed the function to output a single signal and focused on single readout, which inspired us to establish a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-signal sensor for simple and rapid glucose-sensing.

In the present study, we designed a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-signal sensor for H2O2–assisted glucose detection based on Au@Ag NPs and C-dots. The as-prepared Au@Ag NPs were added to the solution of C-dots prepared by hydrothermal method. C-dots were used as efficient energy donors, and the fluorescence was significantly quenched by Au@Ag NPs (silver shell as acceptor). H2O2 and gluconic acid were produced from enzymatic-triggered oxidation of glucose, which etches silver shell (Ag0) into Ag+ and AuNPs was exposed. This results in the absorbance decrease of silver shell, expressed as a distinct color change from orange yellow to pink and fluorescence recovery of C-dots. Thus, a dual-signal glucose sensing is established founded on the recorder of fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption spectra. Compared with the currently existing methods, this strategy exhibited higher sensitivity, also no additional chromogenic agent needs to be added due to the high molar extinction coefficient of AgNPs [25]. Its additional advantages, including direct readout with bare eyes, easy operation as well as reliability in complex samples were important for practical application.

Gold(III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, ≥99.0%), glucose (C6H12O6, ≥99.0%), lactose (C12H22O11, ≥98.0%), galactose (C6H12O6, ≥98.0%), Fructose (C6H12O6, ≥99.0%), trehalose (C12H22O11, ≥99.0%), maltose (C12H22O11·H2O, ≥98.0%), xylose (C5H10O5, ≥99.0%), sucrose (C12H22O11, ≥99.5%), methionine (C5H11NO2S, ≥99.0%), arginine (C6H14N4O2, ≥99.0%), histidine (C6H9N3O2, ≥99.0%), urea (CH4N2O, ≥99.0%) and silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥99.0%) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China http://www.macklin.cn/). KCl, NaCl, and sodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O, ≥99.0%) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemistry Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China, http://www.cdkelong.com/). Glucose oxidase (GOx, 50 ku) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China http://www.shyuanye.com/). All chemicals used were obtained from commercial sources, and no need further purification. In the experiment, ultrapure water used was prepared with distilled water passed through a Millipore system (18.23 MΩ cm, Ulupure, China).

Fluorescence spectra was measured on a PerkinElmer LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (USA) under the following conditions: excitation wavelength was 365 nm, emission range was 370–630 nm, excitation and emission monochromator slit width were 6 nm and 10 nm. UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired by an AnalytikJena Specord200Plus spectrophotometer (Germany) from 300 to 700 nm. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) images, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements, selected area electron diffraction (SAED pattern) and elemental mapping were characterized with a field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL-2100F, Japan, accelerating voltage 300 kV). The hydrodynamic size and Zeta potential experiments of the particles were performed on a Malvern Zetasizer instrument (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano, UK).

C-dots were synthesized by following Rao’s method with some modifications [26]. Typically, 0.50 g citric acid and 0.50 mL ethylene diamine were dissolved in 15 mL purified water to form a transparent solution, which was transferred into a 50 mL 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 6 h in electric heating oven, then cool down to room temperature naturally. Subsequently, the yellow solution obtained was centrifuged at 12000 rpm and the purified C-dots were stored in the refrigerator for further use.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were synthesized according to previous report of the citrate reduction of HAuCl4 [27]. Au@Ag NPs were synthesized following the procedures described previously with minor modification [28]. All glassware used in the course of the experiment were thoroughly washed in aqua regia, repeated cleaned with ultrapure water, dried in electric drying oven before use. 0.5 mL 1% HAuCl4·3H2O was added to 100 mL ultrapure water, then the mixture was transferred to a three-necked flask and heated to boiling with fast stirring. 1 mL (1%, w/w) sodium citrate solution was rapidly added to the boiled HAuCl4·3H2O solution under magnetic stirring, and the mixture was refluxed for 20 min. Finally, the color of solution changed from orange yellow to pink. The synthesized AuNPs were used as seed particles. Then 619 μL AgNO3 solution (1%, w/w) was added to 100 mL boiling AuNPs solution. The final molar ratio of Au: Ag was fixed at 1:3. Subsequently, 536 μL sodium citrate solution (1%, w/w) was added to the reaction solution with vigorous stirring. The resulting solution was then subjected to refluxing for 30 min and then naturally cooled down to room temperature. The synthesized Au@Ag NPs solution was purified through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove the large particulate impurities and placed in the dark for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and UV–vis detection of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: One mL glucose solution at different concentrations (0 μM–400 μM) and 200 μL GOx solution (2 mg mL−1) was added to a transparent penicillin bottle and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min in Tris buffer (pH 7.4, 20 mM). After the reaction was completed, 0.9 mL 6000-fold diluted C-dots and 0.9 mL prepared Au@Ag NPs solution were added to this bottle and the final volume was 3 mL. Finally, the mixture was held at ambient condition for 50 min with vigorous stirring. The solution was transferred into cuvette for fluorescence measurements and UV–vis detection, respectively. The fluorescence measurement was under the following condition: excitation wavelength was 365 nm, emission range was 370–630 nm, excitation and emission monochromator slit width were 6 nm and 10 nm. UV–vis scanning parameters were set from 300 to 700 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose detection in human blood sample
ce:section.ce:para: Human blood samples were obtained from Chengdu First People’s Hospital. Prior to analysis, the blood samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to collect the supernatant. The supernatants were diluted 100 times before use, respectively. First, 500 μL supernatant sample were spiked with 500 μL glucose at different concentrations and 200 μL GOx solution (2 mg mL−1) was added to a transparent penicillin bottle and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min in Tris buffer (pH 7.4, 20 mM). Then, 1.8 mL mixture containing 0.9 mL 6000-fold diluted C-dots and 0.9 mL prepared Au@Ag NPs solution were added to the reaction bottle. After 50 min of stirring, both the fluorescence spectrum intensity and UV–vis absorbance intensity at room temperature were recorded.

C-dots and Au@Ag NPs were obtained as described in Scheme 1 . First of all, C-dots were synthesized with a typical hydrothermal method, simultaneously, Au@Ag NPs were prepared with sodium citrate as a reducing agent. In short, AuNPs seed particles were synthesized by means of sodium citrate reduction of HAuCl4·3H2O, Au@Ag NPs were synthesized by reducing a mixture of silver ion and AuNPs seed particles with sodium citrate, similarly. The Ag+ interacted with the surfaces of AuNPs via functional groups such as carboxylic acid, and the silver shell was formed by reduction reaction. Particularly important is that it can quenched the fluorescence of C-dots effectively due to the report of SPEET [17]. The fluorescence of C-dots recovered when H2O2 etched the silver shell and turned into Ag+ [29,30], at the same time, the color of Au@Ag NPs gradually changed from orange-yellow to pink. The proposed dual-signal strategy for glucose sensing has been established.

We have established the strategy of glucose determination using C-dots and Au@Ag NPs, and the preliminary data in Fig. 1 demonstrated the feasibility of the strategy. As shown in Fig. 1a, it's clear to see that AuNPs and Au@Ag NPs have no fluorescence emission peak, the fluorescence spectrum of the C-dots show maximum emission wavelengths at approximately 450 nm under the excitation at 365 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture of C-dots and Au@Ag NPs was much lower than the single C-dots solution, and when H2O2 was added into the mixture the fluorescence of C-dots was recovered. Fig. 1b showed the absorption spectra of C-dots, AuNPs, Au@Ag NPs, C-dots with Au@Ag NPs and C-dots with Au@Ag NPs after the addition of H2O2. From Fig. 1b, AuNPs, C-dots and Au@Ag NPs exhibited an absorption band peak at 520 nm, 340 nm and 405 nm respectively. C-dots and Au@Ag NPs are independent and does not impact relatively in the absorption spectra, because the absorbance of Au@Ag NPs was almost coincided with the mixture of C-dots and Au@Ag NPs. Finally, as H2O2 was added to the C-dots with Au@Ag NPs, the absorbance at 405 nm decreased due to etching of the AgNPs. Correspondingly, the fluorescence enhancement was observed (green line, Fig. 1a) in the presence of H2O2. Beyond that, we ruled out the possibility that H2O2 directly influence the fluorescence of C-dots (Fig. S1). No significant difference was observed for the fluorescence of C-dots before and after H2O2 was added at different concentrations.

As the most important factor for recovery of fluorescence of C-dots and UV-response, the silver concentration was very important to the reaction system. In order to improve the sensitivity of the reaction system toward H2O2, we optimized the silver concentration for the synthesis of Au@Ag NPs. In Fig. S2, the mixed solution of C-dots with Au@Ag NPs based on different silver concentration were obtained, by monitoring the fluorescence ratio (F/F0) and absorbance ratio (A/A0) of the mixed solution, where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm in the presence and absence of H2O2, A and A0 were the absorbance at 405 nm in the presence and absence of H2O2. The Au and Ag mole ratio was selected as 1:3 to obtain the best absorbance ratio and fluorescence ratio. Based on the optimized Au@Ag NPs, the effect of reaction time on the Au@Ag NPs was investigated, Fig. S3 presented the UV absorbance at different reaction time. UV absorption spectrometry results showed that when the reaction time reached 30 min, the absorbance values were not changed. Thus, the optimal reaction time is selected as 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the proposed senor for glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots, AuNPs and Au@Ag NPs were characterized by TEM, Energy Dispersive (EDS), elemental mapping, DLS, and Zeta potential. In order to confirm the structures of C-dots, TEM and HRTEM were used to characterize the structural characteristics of the C-dots (Fig. 2 a), which indicated that the C-dots were nearly spherical and monodispersed structures. Fig. 2b–c show the trend of structure changes and size distribution of AuNPs before and after coating with Ag shell. Fig. 2d shows the structure of Au@Ag NPs after H2O2 etching, simultaneously. In Fig. 2b–d, the as-prepared AuNPs, Au@Ag NPs and the etched nanoparticles were spherical and well-dispersed with an approximate average diameter of 15 nm, 32 nm, and 17 nm. In Fig. 2c, the core–shell nanostructure of Au@Ag nanoparticle was formed after the reduction reaction, and selected area electron diffraction (SAED pattern) displayed distinct ring patterns which agreed well with the standard d-spacing values of both Au0 and Ag0, which indicated the presence of Au (111) and Ag (200), as well as demonstrating their good crystallinity. Fig. 3 a–c showed the DLS data of AuNPs, Au@Ag NPs and the etched nanoparticles with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 19.15 nm, 43.82 nm and 15.71 nm, respectively. TEM images show the actual size of dried sample, whereas DLS method depicts the hydrodynamic diameter of hydrated state of nanoparticle. Accordingly, the diameter recorded by DLS was higher than that by TEM due to solvent effect in the hydrated state [31]. It is noteworthy that there were two peaks in Fig. 3c, which can be partially explained that during the etching process a small amount of exposed AuNPs were agglomerated.

The trend of structure change was confirmed by the increased and decreased zeta potential with added Ag+ to form the core–shell nanoparticles and silver shell etched after H2O2 addition according to Fig. S4, the zeta potential of Au@Ag NPs decreased obviously compare with AuNPs from −27.1 mV to −62.5 mV, after etched by H2O2, the zeta potential was increased to −42.9 mV, which clearly indicated that the formed Au@Ag NPs have good stability whether before or after etched by H2O2. In order to further characterize the Au@Ag nanostructure, Energy Dispersive analysis and elemental mapping were also performed (Fig. 4 ). EDS spectra (Fig. 4a) showed three elements, i.e. carbon element from C-dots, gold element, and silver element, with the latter two originating from gold core and silver shell, respectively. Elemental mapping, as a more direct evidence of core-shell nanostructure, was employed to confirm the structure of Au@Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 4b). Apparently, silver element and the gold element mainly distributed in the peripheries of the nanoparticles and in the internal of the Au@Ag NPs, respectively, as expected from the core-shell structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity for glucose assay
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitive glucose detection was measured by using with the above optimum conditions discussed in“Synthesis optimization and feasibility analysis part”. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence emission intensity at 450 nm increases with higher concentrations of glucose (0–1000 mM). The fluorescence of C-dots at 450 nm were gradually enhanced with increasing glucose concentration from 0.5 to 1000 μM, a distinguishable fluorescence enhancement occurred under the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. The plot of F/F0-1 against the concentration of glucose displayed an excellent linear response with a fine correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9959) in the range of 2.0–400 μM in Fig. 5b, based on 3δ/S, the detection limit was calculated to be 0.67 μM. Furthermore, with the increasing concentration, the GOx-mediated enzymatic reaction of glucose led to the gradually decrease of the absorbance at 405 nm. The most compelling detection result was visible, with the increase of the concentration of glucose, the color of mixture gradually changed from orange-yellow to pink (Fig. 5c). Notably A/A0 showed a linear response to the concentration of glucose within the confines of 0.50–300 μM (Fig. 5d), and the correlation coefficient and detection limit were 0.9913 and 0.20 μM, respectively. The linear ranges and detection limits of some other reports which based on the properties of biological and chemical substance provides a comparative analysis and presented in Table 1 . Clearly, the present strategy for determination of glucose that we developed has high sensitivity and broad linear range. Compared with the single signal sensor, the present dual signal sensor of fluorescence and UV–vis absorption method made detection results much more convincing.

To validate the selectivity of this strategy for glucose detection, the fluorescence and UV–vis absorbance response of this dual signal sensor in the presence of similar molecules and other common interfering substances were discussed. As shown in Fig. 6 , the glucose concentration was 300 μM, the concentration of potential interfering substances lactose galactose, fructose, trehalose, maltose, xylose, sucrose, methionine, arginine, histidine, urea in the solution was 1000 μM, and K+, Na+ in the solution was 2000 μM. Sugars, amino acid and ion above showed no interference under the same experimental conditions, the fluorescence enhancements (F/F0−1) and absorbance decrease (A0/A-1) of the molecules and ions above were negligible compared to the glucose result. Thus, the proposed method exhibits high selectivity for distinguishing glucose from common substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in blood sample
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the applicability of this approach for glucose detection, the proposed sensor was further assessed with human blood samples. As an evaluation criterion to assess the performance of our method, fresh serum samples were analyzed using the commercial Beckman coulter AU5821 chemistry analyzer at Chengdu First People’s Hospital. Subsequently, a number of known concentrations of glucose were added into the same pretreated blood sample, standard addition experiments were performed and the glucose concentrations were calculated using calibration equation. The results of fluorometric and colorimetric were comparable to those obtained by the commercial chemistry analyzer (Table 2 ). The results indicate that the calculated values were in accordance with the measurements of chemistry analyzer. The recoveries of spiked samples ranged from 96.0-105.7% and 97.0-100.8%, the RSDs of the detections ranged from 0.75%–1.59% and 1.91%–2.97%, respectively. Clearly, the fluorometric and colorimetric method has great potential for glucose detection in blood samples.

In conclusion, we designed a sensor for glucose measurement by fluorometric and colorimetric methods based on the etching effect of H2O2 produced from enzymatic-triggered oxidation of glucose on silver shell. Low detection limitation (0.20 μM and 0.67 μM) and good linear relationship (0.50–300 μM and 2.0–400 μM) were obtained for glucose. Moreover, the sensor exhibited high sensitively, long-term stability, and favorable selectivity base on the specificity of enzymatic oxidation reaction and high extinction coefficient of silver. The dual signal sensor was successfully applied to recognize glucose in blood samples, which indicated the enzymatic-triggered metallization method have great potential for rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of glucose by fluorometric and colorimetric dual detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No.03570113), the Education Department of Sichuan Provincial, PR China (Grant No. 16ZA0039).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.03.060.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

J. Phys. Chem. C Nanomater. Interfaces
The potential of self-assembled, pH-responsive nanoparticles of mPEGylated peptide dendron-doxorubicin conjugates for cancer therapy
Biomaterials
A transition metal carbonyl probe for use in a highly specific and sensitive SERS-based assay for glucose
J. Am. Chem. Soc.
Positively-charged gold nanoparticles as peroxidase mimic and their application in hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection
Chem. Commun.
Mediatorless glucose biosensor and direct electron transfer type Glucose/Air biofuel cell enabled with carbon nanodots
Anal. Chem.
AgNP-DNA@GQDs hybrid: new approach for sensitive detection of H2O2 and glucose via simultaneous AgNP etching and DNA cleavage
Anal. Chem.
Label-free Si quantum dots as photoluminescence probes for glucose detection
Chem. Commun.
Hydrogen peroxide displacing DNA from nanoceria: mechanism and detection of glucose in serum
J. Am. Chem. Soc.
MnO2-nanosheet-modified upconversion nanosystem for sensitive turn-on fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose in blood
ACS Appl. Mater. Inter.
Colorimetric visualization of glucose at the submicromole level in serum by a homogenous silver nanoprism-glucose oxidase system
Anal. Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wei Liu is postgraduate student of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current interests include the development of biosensors and fluorescence sensors.

Fang Ding is an associate Professor of Suzhou Institute of Systems Medicine, Center for Systems Medicine, Institute of Basic Medical Sciences, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences & Peking Union Medical College. Her research focuses on bioanalytical applications of nanomaterials.

Yanying Wang received doctor degree in College of Chemistry, Sichuan University in 2011. Now she is an associate professor of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. Her current interests are modified electrode and bioelectroanalysis.
Lei Mao is College student of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. Her current interests include the development of biosensors and fluorescence sensors.
Ruoxuan Liang is College student of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. Her current interests include the development of biosensors and fluorescence sensors.
Ping Zou is a professor in College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current research interests include the isolation of phytochemicals and the synthesis of natural products.
Xianxiang Wang is a professor in College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current research interests are modified electrode.
Qingbiao Zhao is a professor in East China Normal University, he is a researcher in material science, information storage materials and strongly correlated materials.

Hanbing Rao received doctor degree in College of Chemistry, Sichuan University in 2010. Now he is an associate Professor of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current research interests are the preparation and the bioanalytical applications of nanomaterials.
